title: Design platform-agnostic parallel merge safety

sources.reason: Defines the repository governance workflow, finish criteria, and requirement to solidify user corrections.
Defines Calibrated Human-Agent Trust, Evidence Governance, and the Repository Governance Layer boundary.
Defines the North Star and HCI-oriented principles that the compatibility design must serve.
Defines current Core, platform, diff, verification, and archive evidence boundaries.
Documents the existing merge-base, aggregate PR ownership, and pre-merge verification semantics.
Provides the current GitHub pull-request baseline selection and CI integration that the design must generalize.

problemStatement: AI Cockpit validates Work Items sequentially in the current repository workflow, while adopting teams develop independent changes concurrently across GitHub and GitLab editions with different candidate-merge capabilities.

scenarioCoverage.scenario: GitHub Merge Queue and GitLab Merge Trains use platform-generated candidate commits while sharing one Core verification contract.
evidence: Current make ai-pre-merge and check-ai-pr merge-base semantics

scenarioCoverage.scenario: GitLab Free receives equivalent candidate verification through an isolated native-Git merge and a serialized final merge gate.
evidence: GitLab Free resource_group and protected-branch design

scenarioCoverage.scenario: The design does not turn Cockpit Core into a queue, branch graph, or platform workflow engine.
evidence: Core input boundary limited to candidate commit and target base commit
Queue state remains owned by GitHub, GitLab, or the GitLab compatibility adapter

scenarioCoverage.scenario: Runtime behavior and CI configuration remain unchanged by this documentation-only Work Item.
scenarioCoverage.reason: Implementation is explicitly deferred to a later approved Work Item.

riskAssessment.reason: This Work Item changes only a new design document and its governance records. Runtime merge safety is not modified; the document must nevertheless keep future implementation risks such as misleading platform claims, stale candidate evidence, and incomplete GitLab Free fallback behavior explicit.

executionDecision.reason: The user approved the architecture, the GitLab Free compatibility boundary, and creation of a documentation-only design specification.

preReviewWarnings: Review that the document distinguishes platform-native candidate generation, the GitLab Free compatibility gate, and head-only evidence without overstating guarantees.
Review that the compatibility fallback closes target-branch freshness races through serialization, protected branches, and source/target SHA revalidation.
Review that queue ordering and branch aggregation remain outside Cockpit Core.

checkpointPolicy.reason: Record at least one checkpoint before finishing to reduce mid-task drift.

acceptance: The design document defines one platform-neutral Core interface in which the checked-out tree is the candidate merge and AI_BASE_COMMIT identifies its target-branch baseline.

The design documents native adapters for GitHub pull requests and merge_group events plus GitLab merged-result and merge-train pipelines without adding platform fields to Contract, Summary, or Archive schemas.

The design specifies a GitLab Free fallback that uses an isolated temporary worktree, native git merge, a serialized resource_group gate, protected target branches, source and target SHA revalidation, and a least-privilege merge identity.
The design defines fail-closed outcomes for merge conflicts, stale source or target SHAs, failed project or governance checks, missing merge credentials, and unauthorized target-branch mutation.
The design distinguishes native, synthetic, and head-only evidence strengths and never presents head-only validation as parallel merge safety.
The design records the HCI constraint that safe platform capability gaps should be absorbed by compatibility adapters rather than exposed to adopters solely to preserve architectural purity.
No runtime scripts, tests, templates, CI configuration, Makefile targets, schemas, existing architecture or philosophy documents, or archived Work Items are changed by this Work Item.
guidelines: Distinguish current implemented behavior from proposed future behavior and do not claim that the design is already shipped.
Keep Cockpit Core platform-neutral; platform event parsing, queue semantics, credentials, and merge execution belong to adapters.
Prefer native platform candidate generation, provide a safe native-Git fallback where required, and fail closed when candidate freshness cannot be established.

intent.problem: AI Cockpit currently demonstrates sequential merge verification, but real teams create independent Work Items in parallel. GitHub can provide merge-group candidates, GitLab Premium and Ultimate can provide merge-train candidates, and GitLab Free cannot. Without a platform-neutral design and a safe fallback, adopters either lose concurrency safety or must invent their own governance integration.
constraints: One Work Item maps to one branch and one pull or merge request; Git and the hosting platform remain responsible for branch topology, ordering, conflict resolution, and aggregation.
Contract, Summary, Archive, and Cockpit Core must not gain GitHub- or GitLab-specific queue state.

When a hosting platform lacks candidate-merge capabilities, the compatibility adapter must absorb the gap with native Git and CI controls when safety can be established; architectural purity must not override the HCI North Star.
GitLab Free safety must address the race between candidate verification and final merge, not merely test a synthetic merge against a target SHA that may later become stale.
This Work Item produces only the approved design document and governance evidence; implementation, templates, CI configuration, scripts, and tests remain out of scope.
intent.rationale: A small platform-neutral verification contract preserves AI Cockpit as a Repository Governance Layer, while native platform adapters and a Git-based GitLab Free fallback give adopters equivalent evidence without forcing a paid hosting tier or duplicating Git semantics inside Core.

restrictedWriteApproval.reason: Set only when a human explicitly approves restricted governance paths.

rollbackNote: Revert this Work Item diff and restore related tests and docs.
